data.title: Simon Property Group: The Rebirth Of Malls Is Experiential
data.description:
Overview

I previously covered Simon Property Group (NYSE:SPG) back in June and issued a buy rating due to the stock remaining undervalued and the dividend being supported with a large cushion by the current earnings. Since that prior article, SPG has provided a total return that outpaces the S&P 500, accompanied by a recent dividend raise by a modest 2.5%. Simon Property Group operates as a real estate investment trust that generates its earnings from a portfolio of different properties, primarily consisting of malls and shopping outlets.

Ever since the start of the pandemic in 2020, I've commonly read that malls are dying. While I would agree that they do not generally hold the same value as they once did a generation ago, I believe that the landscape of what we consider a mall is changing. Therefore, we should change our perspective of these sorts of properties to really understand why there is still a ton of value to be captured from a REIT like SPG. Over the last decade, SPG has struggled to gain any positive price momentum, further delayed by the drop in 2020. However, I believe that SPG is one of those unique businesses that shouldn't be defined by its past performance, and we should instead look to the future.

The total return over the last decade has been supported by consistent dividends, with a yield now sitting around 4.7%. The dividend continues to be well-supported by the strong financials of SPG. I believe that the dividend growth will become more attractive over time, making SPG a great choice for investors that also value a reliable stream of income from REIT exposure. I wanted to first start this off by taking a look into the current portfolio strength of the REIT and then dive into some thoughts of the changing landscape of mall properties and how SPG will likely capitalize on these shifts.

Portfolio Strength & Financials

Part of SPG's strength comes from its diverse portfolio of properties that are spread across the US, as well as a sizeable amount of international exposure. The latest supplemental data confirms that Florida is their biggest market, accounting for 19.1% of net operating income. This is followed by exposure to the California and Texas markets, accounting for 13.9% and 10.2% respectively. We can also see that US-based malls and outlets are what make up 71.6% of their earnings power.

The revenue streams are so large for Simon that they break off their business into four separate segments. The US malls and premium outlets are the largest sources of revenue. The following segment is referred to as 'The Mills', which are typically value oriented outlets that shoppers can visit to find more attractive sales. The international segment is self-explanatory and consists of properties that are mainly within different regions of Europe and Asia. The last segment is referred to as 'TRG', which represents the acquisition of the Taubman Realty Group that was completed back in 2020.

SPG reported their Q2 earnings back at the start of August and the results were solid. FFO per share came in at $2.90 while revenue landed at $1.46B for the quarter. The level of earnings continues to be strong, driven by the consistent occupancy levels that have trended upward since the pandemic. Occupancy at the end of June sat at 95.6%, representing an increase of 0.9% year over year. As occupancy increases, SPG has been able to capitalize on a growing base minimum rent per square foot of $57.94. This is a 3% increase from the year prior base minimum rent per square foot of $56.27.

A rising occupancy level isn't only great for total earnings potential, but also serves as an indicator that there is a sizeable amount of demand still for these retail spaces. Not to mention, SPG has been able to achieve this growing occupancy during a time of elevated interest rates and higher inflation levels that has negatively impacted consumer spending levels.

Speaking of higher interest rates, SPG currently has debt totaling $23.2B. While this is an admittedly large amount of debt, the total debt burden has modestly improved from the $24.9B in long-term debt that was owed at the end of 2022. As debt decreases, the cash position has increased and strengthened the overall quality of SPG's balance sheet. SPG current has $1.23B in cash and equivalents that can help them navigate future headwinds, reinvest back into the growth of their business, or go towards dividends and share repurchases. Thankfully, SPG's credit ratings are strong and pose no current vulnerability to the financial health.

However, I do remain a bit cautious as I believe that the top tenants can be a source of weakness. For instance, The Gap (GAP) is their largest tenant and accounts for 2.8% of SPG's annual base rent. GAP has strong financials at the moment with a healthy balance of cash and debt, but have a growing list of store closures since 2020, now sitting over 350 locations. Similarly, Signet Jewelers Limited (SIG) plans to close over 150 locations.

However, I don't mention these two businesses by name because I think they are inherently weak companies. I mention these names because they all share a similar path of seeing an increase percentage of their sales coming from online sources. At some point, we have to question the longevity of these physical locations and whether or not they are actually a requirement in this modern era of consumer spending.

A lot of these concerns are outweighed by the fact that management continues to allocate new dollars towards different growth initiatives such as new development projects. For instance, SPG committed $1.12B towards new developments and redevelopment projects through FY25, which is expected to generate a stabilized return of 8%. Redevelopments within the mall segment accounted for $868M, making up the largest bulk of their investment. These new developments can help SPG capitalize on a changing retail landscape, further strengthened by the recent announcement of a $3.5B revolving credit facility.

Changing Landscape

It isn't shocking news that the habits of consumers are changing. As expected, online sales have consistently grown to account for a larger percentage of total retail sales over the last few years. Estimates by Insider Intelligence indicate that the share of ecommerce sales are expected to grow to 20.6% of total retail sales by the end of 2027, up from the estimated 16.6% for the end of 2024. As a larger percentage of sales shift to online sources, I believe that SPG will shift their tenant exposure and account for these changes.

More specifically, I believe that malls will slowly shift to include a lot more experiential based tenants over time to offset this vulnerability. This would include embracing certain experienced based businesses like movie theaters, bowling alleys, a greater emphasis on dining experiences, and other sort of recreational based tenants. If you are a frequent mall-goer, you've likely already noticed this shift in some of the popular malls in your area.

Although not related to SPG specifically, a real-world example of this sort of experiential focus would be the local American Dream mall based in NJ. This mall aims to offer a one of a kind experience with the inclusion of an amusement park, mini-golf park, indoor waterpark, and an actual indoor ski slope. Times are changing and there is plenty of data that suggests millennials and Gen Z prefer to spend their money on experiences as opposed to material items. A recent example of SPG taking part of this change would be the deal they struck with 'Camp' to open two new locations that serve to attract families looking to have fulfilling experiences.

A retail forecast compiled by Retail Strategies seems to agree with this exact assessment on the outlook of malls. We can see that they estimate the strongest categories for growth comes from and experiential sectors. In this scenario, it includes experiences such as eGaming, virtual reality, culture exhibits, and other competitive socializing. The competitive socializing area would include finding tenants that support spaces like gyms for basketball games, courts for pickleball or tennis, and creative businesses like escape rooms.

The most recent example of SPG implementing some of these changes would be from their main site where they have a breakdown of different experiences they are currently looking to expand. However, I look forward to announcements of new developments in the experience space over FY25. Failure to adapt to these experiential based tenants can be a source of vulnerability.

Risk Profile

If SPG fails to adapt to changing consumer habits, earnings will be negatively impacted over time. This is assuming that some of the retail tenants start to close locations and shift their business models to incorporate a greater online presence. Empty stores would essentially hurt their bottom line for annual base rent collection and SPG may have to offer lower rents to attract new tenants to replace these tenants. This could cause occupancy levels to decrease over time, which may also lower investor confidence.

A great example of a peer REIT that comes to mind would be EPR Properties (EPR), which has a portfolio of different experiential based tenants. While this REIT's structure and strategy isn't necessarily mall-based like SPG's I think their list of tenants would be a good starting point that SPG could use as a reference. EPR has exposure to experienced based tenants such as movie theaters, eat & play tenants that offer a gaming and food experience, as well as amusement park attractions. If SPG fails to adapt and get an edge in this area, investor capital could transition into peers like EPR that are better suited to capitalize on the shift.

Another potential risk could be a possible change in management sometime down the line. It was announced earlier in the year that the CEO, David Simon, was undergoing cancer treatment. David will still serve as CEO of the business for the time being, and his son has been elected to serve as a Senior Vice President of Corporate Investments. While I certainly wish David a speedy recovery, the possibility of a change in management could be something we see happening eventually.

Dividend

As of the latest declared quarterly dividend of $2.05 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 4.7%. As a divided investor, I really value that SPG's dividend coverage currently sits at a rate that allows me to sleep well at night. As previously mentioned, FFO came in at $2.90 per share over the last quarter. This means that the earnings cover the dividend at a comfortable rate of 141.4%, which means that SPG can continue to be a core holding that continually compounds my annual dividend income with each passing quarter. In addition, this strong coverage allows there to be some optimism when it comes to future dividend growth.

It's also reassuring that management reinforced this positive outlook on a recent global real estate conference with Bank of America, by confirming that free cash flow levels are strong even after distributing dividend payments out to shareholders.

Net debt to EBITDA is about 5.2 times. The balance sheet is well poised to continue to support our growth going forward. The company generates about $1.5 billion of free cash flow after paying its dividend annually that's available for reinvestment in its properties, which is a rather unique situation relative to other REITs in the world. - Brian McDade, Chief Financial Officer

While the dividend did previously get reduced back in 2020, this was driven by the weakness of lower sales volumes at their locations as a result of the lockdowns during the pandemic. Therefore, I do not hold this cut against management and fully understand the reasoning to do so in an effort to preserve value. Since then, the dividend growth has been meaningful, considering that the yield is already sufficient. For instance, the dividend has increased at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 5.13% over the last five-year period.

The divided was cut to a quarterly rate of $1.30 per share during the pandemic in 2020. This has since increased to a quarterly total of $2.05 per share, representing an increase of 57.7%. If SPG continues to grow at an attractive rate like this, it will improve investor yield on cost. This adds to the appeal of using SPG as an income compounding vehicle. Since the coverage rate is so strong, this creates a 'sleep well at night' appeal where investors do not have to be concerned on whether or not any headwinds will reduce the income generated from this position.

However, it's worth noting that the distributions received from SPG are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have less favorable tax consequences than the qualified dividends that you'd receive from more traditional dividend paying stocks. Based on your own situation, it may be worth it to considering placing an SPG position inside of a tax advantaged account to offset this tax burden.

Valuation Outlook

SPG currently trades at a price to AFFO ratio of 14.43x, undercutting the sector median price to AFFO ratio of 15.64x. This may indicate that SPG is trading at a discount to its peers and is an attractive opportunity to accumulate shares. Wall St. seems to be a bit more pessimistic with their outlook based on the average price target of $167.71 per share, which represents a slight downside potential of about 4% from the current price level. However, I remain a bit more optimistic with my outlook because of the favorable interest rate environment ahead.

As previously mentioned, SPG currently holds $24.9B of long-term debt on their balance sheet. This debt is held at a weighted average interest rate of 3.75% and the majority of this debt has a maturity runway between six or seven years. As SPG relies heavily on debt to finance new developments and other growth initiatives, future interest rate cuts will provide some relief for any of this debt that has been taken on a floating rate basis.

The Fed has already implemented their first interest rate cut of 50 basis points in mid-September. Optimism and anticipation around interest rate cuts have boosted the real estate sector up a bit, and I expect there to be additional rate cuts to follow. Lower interest rates directly translate to a lower cost of debt for a business like SPG, so I believe cuts to serve as a strong positive catalyst for growth over the next twelve months.

Recently economic data such as an upward trending unemployment rate could contribute to the likelihood of another rate cut. The unemployment rate now sits at 4.1% and as more households are out of work, consumer spending levels may temporarily drop or become more geared towards SPG's 'Mill' segment where shoppers can find a greater variety of deals. At the same time, inflation levels have consistently trended downward of the last six months and can provide another group of consumers some relief.

Inflation now sits at 2.4% and, while remaining higher than the Fed's 2% target, serves as a sign of relief for consumers. As some of the higher income consumers see relief, this can simultaneously boost the traffic levels that the higher-end luxury tenants see. It especially helps that we are now in Q4 and heading towards the holiday spending season. The combination of these factors can be enough to incentivize the Fed to begin reducing interest rates.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating for Simon Property Group as occupancy levels are strong and support a healthy FFO level. Earnings currently support the dividend with a wide margin of safety and lead to an increased likelihood of future dividend rises. The portfolio of properties remains diverse in nature and I believe that SPG has an opportunity to continue its growing momentum by shifting a focus towards experiential based properties. Data supports the notion that Millennials and Gen Z would prefer to spend their money on experiences rather than material items and SPG can use this as an opportunity for growth. I believe that the valuation still remains attractive since the price to AFFO ratio remains under the sector median and interest rate cuts can serve as a positive price catalyst for growth.

data.title: Ally Financial: Shares Are Unlikely To Rebound Until Delinquencies Stabilize
data.description:
While shares of Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY) have been a strong performer over the past year, up 48%, their recent performance has been poor as investor concern about rising credit costs intensifies. Q3 earnings results have done little to assuage these fears, given higher charge-offs. I last covered Ally in July, downgrading shares from a “buy” to “hold,” but with the stock down 20%, a downgrade all the way to “sell” would have been justified. With new financials and shares having underperformed, now is a good time to revisit ALLY.

In the company’s third quarter, Ally earned $0.93 in adjusted EPS, which actually beat consensus by $0.43. However, there were $0.57 of tax benefits, which likely made its reported EPS not comparable to consensus. Simply put, this quarter was not materially better than expectations, as the company faces higher credit costs and disappointing interest margins.

First, as I discussed in July, there is some accounting noise around tax incentives for electric vehicle (EV) leases that reduce Ally’s tax bill today, inflating earnings. These savings are then passed on to consumers over the course of the lease, so it is entirely a timing issue. In Q3, there were $179 million in EV lease tax credits. $107 million was related to Q3 volumes, and there was a $72 million true-up from H1 activity. Because it books the tax gain first, it then records less interest income, a 6bp headwind to reported net interest margin (NIM). Because of this accounting treatment, Ally had a -53% adjusted tax rate, which speaks to how much EVs are distorting it given a corporate tax rate of 21%.

Alongside results, Ally said it was considering changing its accounting method to no longer recognize the tax gain all at once, instead deferring it over the life of the loan. This does not change the life of the loan’s profitability, but it will reduce book value by $309 million, as any change will be retroactive. While I believe this change would improve the comparability of earnings, there would be a 20bp capital hit, given the $309 million reversal.

Moving away from accounting details and to actual results, ALLY continues to gradually shrink its loan book. Total loans and leases fell $900 million to $146.2 billion, with yields down 3bps to 8.19%. Risk weighted assets are down $5 billion over the past year, helping to drive a 50bp increase in its common equity tier 1 capital ratio (CET1).

Because of shrinking loans, ALLY can allow some of its higher-cost deposits to mature. On an end of period basis, deposits fell by $600 million, given a smaller loan book. In Q3, it had $152.2 billion in average deposits, down 0.1% sequentially and about $1.3 billion from last year. Management has actively let deposits decline, given a drop in loans.

Given Ally’s strong history of deposit accumulation, I am comfortable with this modest pullback, especially as loan growth is likely to be muted in the current consumer credit environment. Plus, even with slightly lower deposits, Ally’s liquidity position has continued to improve as it has a 96% loan to deposit ratio from 98% last year.

While its trend in deposits makes sense, I was disappointed that the average cost rose 2bps to 4.23%. I would have hoped to see a more meaningful improvement from the Fed rate cut. Because deposit costs have proven stickier, Ally reported a 3.25% net interest margin, down 1bp from last year and down 5bps sequentially.

Now, Ally did cut deposit rates 20bps in September after the Fed rate cut, but this likely occurred too late in the quarter to impact results. Still, this was just 40% of the Fed’s move, whereas Ally aims to pass 70% on to customers. Consequently, Ally sees NIM “choppy” based on competitive trends for deposits and expects “slow” deposit betas in the near-term. Combined with $65 billion of floating rate asset exposure, this has pressured NIM, leading Ally to cut 2024 guidance, even as it still sees 4% over the medium term.

Beyond rate pressures, credit has been a focus, playing a larger role in the share price decline over the past two months. Ally took $645 million in provisions for credit losses, up $137 million from last year. This was also up from $457 million in Q2. Charge-offs in Q3 underperformed expectations, as the benefit from vintage shift has not been as large as hoped. Indeed, they rose 24bps sequentially to 1.5%.

For much of the past year, I have written about credit normalization, with losses worsening from extremely low levels but eventually settling out. To this point, we are seeing the pace of worsening decelerate. Charge-offs are up 39bps YoY from a peak of 118bps. However, the fact that they are still rising, alongside delinquencies, is disappointing.

As you can see below, delinquencies rose 18bps to 4.51%. Most concerningly, 90+ day delinquencies rose 8bps to 0.59%. These are the delinquencies most likely to result in defaults and charge-offs. That continued rise likely means credit costs will stay higher for longer.

Because of worse credit performance, Ally has added to reserves. It now has 3.8% coverage of auto reserves to auto loans, up 15bps, due to Hurricane Helene-related losses and a slower improvement in charge offs. $3.2 billion of its $3.7 billion in reserves are for its auto lending business. Indeed, if there was one bright spot, its smaller credit card division saw improvement with charge-offs down 270bps to 9.9%.

Ally has taken action to tighten credit standards. Over the past two years, its auto loan approval rate has declined from 35% to 28% with an average FICO score of 710, up 22 points. Its borrower’s average payment to income ratio is down 1.4% to 8.3%. As such, credit costs should improve as it moves past its 2022 vintage. As you can see below, credit trends are better for 2023 loans than 2022 with 2024 better than 2023 at this point. While the improvement has been incremental, these shifts do support a peak in credit costs, likely sometime next year, about a year later than I initially expected.

Importantly, Ally does remain well capitalized, with a 9.8% CET1 capital ratio. This is above its long-term 9-9.5% target. However, with the EV accounting change likely to be a 0.2% hit and a 19bp impact to capital in Q1 from CECL accounting phase-in, capital is a pro forma 9.4%. This is still solid, but I do not expect capital returns outside its 3.5% dividend until 2026 at the soonest.

As you can see below, Ally has adjusted its expectations for 2024. Full-year guidance is for a 10bp lower NIM and 5bp higher charge-off rate, with retail auto charge-offs up 15-20bps. Essentially, it is making a bit less on rates and facing more credit costs. This double impact has been a major headwind for shares.

With NIM likely to run about 10bps narrower than I previously expected and credit costs taking longer to normalize, I am looking for $3.50-$4.00 in EPS over the next year from $4-$4.50. I have viewed 11x as a full forward multiple given its higher-risk loan book, but with delinquencies still rising, shares may stay closer to 10x. ALLY has a $36.43 adjusted tangible book value, and I view 1-1.1x as a fair valuation until we see delinquencies stabilize.

These various valuation metrics each point to about $35-37.50 in as a fair value, or 2-8% of upside. In other words, I do think the recent sell-off has been largely justified by fundamentals, and risks are relatively well priced. However, until we see credit trends stabilize, shares are likely to have limited upside. If shares fell toward $33 or 10% below book value, I would likely move to a buy. But for now, I remain a hold given limited upside and disappointing recent operating performance.

data.title: VXX: Sell Volatility Ahead Of The Election (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
All eyes are on the US general election which is now less than two weeks away. The S&P 500’s uptrend appears to have paused with uncertainty around who will be the next POTUS still running high despite former President Trump’s chances to win turning more favorable in the betting markets.

All the while, the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) keeps hovering around the 20 mark. That's a level close to the long-term average, but it’s unusual to see such a VIX print when the SPX is near an all-time high. But it makes sense when you consider the VIX’s mechanics – the VIX is priced based on the premiums for put and call options on the US large cap index looking out 30 calendar days. With the election now within that window, a high VIX indicates some unease around what might happen to stocks after the first Tuesday in November.

A recent report from Goldman Sachs, however, points out that it’s common to see implied volatility readings tick higher ahead of the general election date, but then subside thereafter. I assert that the long-term pattern is likely to play out.

Hence, I have a sell rating on the iPath Series B S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures ETN (BATS:VXX). I had a near-term buy rating on the ETN back in January, so this is technically a downgrade. As a broad warning, given the steep contango often priced into VIX futures contracts, it’s generally a bad strategy to hold VXX beyond a few weeks.

Goldman Sachs: VIX Usually Drops Post-Election

According to the issuer, VXX is designed to provide exposure to the S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures TM Index Total Return Index. The ETNs are riskier than ordinary unsecured debt securities and have no principal protection. The ETNs are unsecured debt obligations of the issuer, Barclays Bank PLC, and are not, either directly or indirectly, an obligation of or guaranteed by any third party. Any payment to be made on the ETNs, including any payment at maturity or upon redemption, depends on the ability of Barclays Bank PLC to satisfy its obligations as they come due. An investment in the ETNs involves significant risks, including possible loss of principal, and may not be suitable for all investors.

VXX is a small ETP with just $281 million in assets under management, and that’s about the same as its $279 million size when I last analyzed VXX in December 2023. The product has a high cost, too, given its annual expense ratio of 0.89%. There is no dividend yield and share-price momentum has been very poor in the last year.

VXX’s price has held in there since the August volatility spike, perhaps due to a near-term backwardation in VIX futures. The ETN is decently liquid given high average daily volume of more than 6 million shares, and its median 30-day bid/ask spread is rather tight at just four basis points as of October 23, 2024.

Let’s home in on this whole “VIX term structure” thing. In basic terms, the “backwardated” curve simply suggests that traders expect volatility in the short term but then calmer conditions looking out beyond a month. For us, we should take this as a sign that there is a significant premium put on near-dated VIX futures and products that track it.

Since VXX owns November and December contracts today, it is priced richly. If the usual pattern plays out, it could be like the air being released from a balloon after November 5. Bear in mind, though, that there is a Fed decision in the afternoon following the general election.

VIX Futures Curve: A November Premium

Another revealing indicator on the volatility front is the less-talked-about 9-day Volatility Index. It is merely in the mid-teens compared to the VIX index near 20.

It further underscores that volatility is pinned to the election – not any events over the coming 9 trading days (as of October 23, 2024).

9-Day VIX Is Low, Implied Volatility is Focused on November 5-6

Bigger picture, November is the worst month for the VIX, according to the last 20 years of seasonal data. (The VIX was first constructed in 1990.) That makes the second month of Q4 the worst month on the calendar.

Interestingly, December and January can be sneaky-high volatility periods, so my VXX sell recommendation is primarily focused on the next five weeks.

VIX Seasonality: Bearish November Trends

The Technical Take

I wouldn’t put a ton of weight into VXX’s technicals, but they are worth peering into. Notice in the chart below that VXX has a declining long-term 200-day moving average, which suggests that the bears control the primary trend. But price action since July has actually been strong (I mentioned earlier that the VIX’s backwardated term structure could be a boon to VXX).

Also take a look at the uptrend support line that dates back more than three months, while upside resistance is seen in the mid to high $50s. With a high amount of volume by price between $40 and $60, the current zone could hold up through early November.

Overall, the technicals are neutral, which is rather impressive for a decaying product like VXX.

VXX: Uptrend Since the Summer, Neutral RSI Trends, $58 Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a sell rating on VXX. While fears seem to be growing ahead of the election, historical data reveal that the post-election stretch is often bearish for volatility. What’s more, despite decent technicals right now, November has been the worst month for the VIX going back two decades.

data.title: Kimberly-Clark: Not Convinced Of Meeting FY 2024 Guidance
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Kimberly-Clark Corp. (NYSE:KMB) in May'24, in which I recommended a sell rating due to my expectation that the market was too optimistic on the business performance given that volume growth has decelerated significantly (on a four-year stack basis), this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I stay sell-rated on KMB as I expect OSG and earnings growth to come in weaker than what management guided, due to the poor consumer spending backdrop.

Investment thesis

KMB released its 3Q24 earnings yesterday, which saw net sales of $4.95 billion, gross profit of $1.815 billion, adj EBIT of $810 million, and adj earnings of $577 million. Net sales growth decelerated for the 3rd straight quarter, falling by 3.5% this quarter vs. 2% last year. On an organic sales growth [OSG] basis, KBM reported 1% OSG growth, driven primarily by pricing growth. Hence, on a like-for-like basis, OSG growth is likely to be negative without the strong pricing environment. By segment, Personal Care [PC] saw 3% OSG; Consumer Tissue [CT] saw -1% OSG; and K-C Professional [KCP] saw -1% OSG.

It seems that the market has finally started to realize the weak fundamentals of KMB as the stock fell sharply by 4.5% yesterday post-earnings, and I think there is still more room to fall. To start, I would like to comment about the 1% OSG this quarter. I believe this 1% OSG does not accurately reflect the growth of the business. There are three elements in this growth equation: price, mix, and volume. From the earnings slide, we know that mix is not a major contributor. This leaves us with price and volume. Volume is the best indicator of demand, in my opinion, and KMB continues to report negative volume growth (on a 2/3/4-year stack basis). Even on a one-year basis, volume growth is not positive. What really drives the 1% OSG is pricing, which seems positive at face value since KMB was able to raise prices despite poor volume, indicating some form of pricing power. I don't think this is true. If we split OSG performance by region, it is distinctively different between developed and developing markets. The latter saw low-single-digit decline vs. developing markets that saw 8% growth. The 8% growth was possible due to the hyperinflationary environment. As such, my sense is that the entire 1% OSG is driven by the inflation environment in developed markets. Absent this inflation benefit, OSG is likely to be in the low-single-digit range (reflecting the developed markets' OSG performance).

Despite this, management still expects 3-4% OSG for FY24. While this is a slight downward revision from the previous mid-single-digit OSG expectation, I think KMB is going to come in at the low end of this guide with the potential of even missing it. As a reference, OSG on a year-to-date basis is 3.7%; for KMB to meet the midpoint of this guide, 4Q24 needs to deliver OSG of 2.8%. This implies KMB to see massive sequential OSG acceleration from 1% to 2.8%, which I find unlikely due to a few reasons.

Firstly, the entire 1% OSG in 3Q24 was due to inflationary pricing (Argentina is the major cause of this). With Argentina's inflation coming down rapidly, it seems unlikely that KMB can raise prices even higher. Secondly, the volume trend remains weak (still negative on a 2/3/4-year stack), and I don't see a major catalyst or reason for it to inflect higher as the consumer spending environment remains poor. The consumer confidence index has dipped in October, lending rates remain high at >6% (went up recently), and commentaries from consumer-facing businesses echo a weak demand environment (beers and spirits companies, and Starbucks).

This poor consumer spending backdrop directly impacts retailers' willingness to stock up on inventories, which impacts KMB sell-in rates. Management has also noted that the shipment spikes on recently tracked channel data will not be any material tailwinds. As such, if we combine the view that volume is unlikely to recover sharply and push prices higher, it seems incredibly challenging for OSG to accelerate by 180 bps sequentially.

Down the P&L, management is also guiding FY24 adj. EBIT to grow mid-to-high teens on a constant currency basis and adj. EPS to grow mid-to-high teens on a constant currency basis. For reference, year-to-date adj EBIT grew ~11% and adj EPS grew 14%. With my view that OSG acceleration is unlikely to happen, I don't think management profit guidance can be easily achieved as well. Note that KMB plans to continue the momentum in investments back into the brands/operations, and this is going to put pressure on 4Q24 adj EBIT/EPS.

Valuation

Using the same model mechanics as I did previously, I laid out the potential upside if KMB achieves management's guidance. Even if we assume KMB grows adj EPS at 11% (reported basis) and FY25 to see normalized adj EPS growth (ignoring the fact that there should be a period of recovery as consumers are unlikely to just revert back to normalized spending behaviors) and trades at the historical 10-year average of 19x forward PE, there is only 4% upside from here.

On the flip side, if KMB misses the EPS guide, which it has potential to do given my view that 4Q24 OSG is not going to accelerate as much as expected and step up in investments to pressure 4Q24 margin profile, the market is likely to further punish the stock by derating it. KMB now trades at 18x forward PE (1x below its 10-year average). If we comp KMB against Unilever, which is expected to grow earnings by mid-teens one-year forward and is trading at ~18.5x forward PE today, I think it is fair to assume that the market could potentially derate KMB lower than 18x. Using the KMB 10-year trading range, the -1 standard deviation is 17x.

Risk

Consumers' spending behavior may recover faster than expected, causing retailers to rush to stock up inventories. This will result in a strong sell-in motion for KMB, driving a surge in volume growth in 4Q24. This will easily help KMB to meet its OSG and earnings guidance and even potentially beat it.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for KMB is a sell rating. The poor consumer spending environment is a big hurdle for KMB to meet its full-year guidance. While KMB was able to print 1% OSG in 3Q24, the reality is it was driven by pricing in the hyperinflationary developing countries and not volume-driven. Given the potential for further downside in earnings and valuation, I recommend investors remain cautious.

data.title: Tesla Q3: Positive Outlook For EV Recovery
data.description:
I reiterated a ‘Strong Buy’ rating on Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) (NEOE:TSLA:CA) in my previous article published in September 2024, discussing their in-house 4680 battery technology. The market was disappointed by Tesla’s Robotaxi event on October 10th as the company didn’t present significant progress in their FSD and robotics technology. Despite these near-term disappointments, I remain confident that Tesla’s FSD will benefit from their massive AI training. Tesla delivered a robust Q3 result beating the market expectations. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $303 per share.

Disappointing Robotaxi Event and NHTSA Probe

Tesla hosted their Robotaxi event on October 10th and Elon Musk revealed that their robotaxi will be available by 2027 at a price under $30,000. In addition, they presented their humanoid Optimus robot and projected the robot will cost around $20,000 to $30,000 over time. It is reported that these robots were remotely controlled by humans during the event. Overall, I think the market was disappointed with the Robotaxi event, and Tesla has not proved any significant progress in their FSD technology as well as AI trainings.

As discussed in my previous article, I am confident that Tesla can leverage their massive real-road driving data to perform AI training, and the next version of FSD will be powered by AI inference capabilities.

On October 18th, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) started to probe the failure of Tesla’s FSD equipped EVs, covering about 2.40 million vehicles that include 2016-2024 Model S and X, 2017-2024 Model 3, 2020-2024 Model Y, 2023-2024 Cybertruck equipped with FSD, as reported by the media.

Interestingly, it is not the first time NHTSA initiated a probe into Tesla’s autonomous driving software. In 2021, NHTSA opened a probe into Tesla’s Autopilot software due to its repeated collisions with parked emergency vehicles. As a result, the agency pushed Tesla to recall more than 2 million vehicles, nearly all the vehicles it had sold at that time.

The government probe is a long process, taking several years. I guess Tesla might need a recall again sometime in the near future. If it is just software updates, Tesla could easily offer a software update to solve the whole problem. Overall, I think the NHTSA probe is manageable.

Q3 and Outlook

Tesla released its Q3 result on October 23rd after the market closed, reporting 7.8% revenue growth and 24.1% adjusted EBITDA growth, as shown in the chart below.

My biggest takeaway from the quarter is that Tesla expects slight growth in vehicle deliveries for FY24, demonstrating a moderate market recovery in the EV market. As shown in the chart below, Tesla reported a positive growth in production and delivery during the quarter, following two consecutive quarters of decline.

For the near-term growth, I continue to believe Tesla will sustain 25% revenue growth for the following reasons:

  • Automotive: Tesla has made significant progress in its Cybertruck production ramp, which could potentially contribute significant growth to their overall production and delivery. I think Tesla’s differentiation comes from their future AI-based FSD software and in-house battery technologies. I project their automotive business growing in line with overall EV growth, delivering 23% year-over-year growth from FY25 onwards.
  • Energy & Storage: I continue to forecast energy transformation will drive the business growth in Energy & Storage. I project the segment will grow by 50% in the near-term, considering the business is still in the infant stage.
  • Services: I model the business sustain their 20% growth momentum, aligned with their recent trends.

Putting together, I calculate Tesla can sustain 25% growth momentum in the near future.

I continue to model 90bps margin expansion for the DCF model. As discussed in my previous article, their in-house battery technology could potentially improve their gross margin over time. Tesla is still in the process of ramping the productions for Cybertruck and Model 3. With the production ramping up, I assume the company will begin experimenting margin expansion from the economy of scales. I project 60bps gross margin improvement and 30bps operating leverage from SG&A.

Due to the recent NHTSA probe, I set aside $100 million for the potential recall and penalties.

The cost of equity is calculated to be 16.8% assuming: risk-free rate 3.6%; beta 1.89; equity risk premium 7%.

With these assumptions, the free cash flow from equity (FCFE) can be calculated as followings:

Discounting all the future FCFE, the one-year target price is calculated to be $303 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

While Elon Musk forecasts that robotaxi will be available by 2027, the robotaxi still has lots of hurdles including road safety policy changes, autonomous driving technology, insurance, and other potential third-party liabilities. In addition, the product launch of robotaxi will be dependent on Tesla’s AI-based FSD technology. In the near future, Tesla might postpone the timeline for their robotaxi launch, which could pose near-term pressure on their stock price.

Verdict

While Tesla’s Robotaxi event was a bit disappointing to the market, I am confident that Tesla is making progress in their FSD AI training. Tesla provided a positive outlook for their vehicle production and delivery for FY24, which could become a major catalyst for their stock price. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $303 per share.

data.title: Alibaba: Buy This Little Dip
data.description:
My Thesis Update

I was fortunate to initiate my coverage of Alibaba Group (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF) stock at the end of January 2024, during its lowest local price point. So in a way, I luckily managed to catch the local bottom for BABA stock at the time, and since then, it has grown by 51.5%, whereas the S&P 500 index (SPX) has increased by almost 22.4%. In my last bullish update on BABA, when the stock had only risen 8.8% since I began my coverage, I called for buying the dip as I believed Alibaba remained one of the most undervalued yet highly attractive opportunities available at that time. I saw a promising catalyst on the horizon that could "represent a turning point for Alibaba and the entire Chinese stock market." And so it happened - China's party leadership announced massive measures to stimulate the economy, enabling Chinese equities to make a huge comeback. Against all this backdrop, BABA stock couldn't help but react with a sharp price rise:

Recently, however, we have seen the stock's sharp rise followed by a slight decline - the relief of the rally began in early October and continues to this day, with BABA stock down about 14.5% off its 2024 high:

However, I don't believe that this relief will last long: In my opinion, BABA should continue to rise as investors return to China and the economy and the company's diverse business advantages show signs of a sustained recovery. The upcoming Q2 numbers shall provide an additional catalyst to convince investors that BABA deserves buying. Hence, my "Buy" rating today, with a call to buy this little dip.

My Reasoning

Alibaba Group's Q1 FY2025 report (calendar Q2 2024) revealed mixed signals, with modest growth and resurgent challenges. The firm generated RMB 243.2 billion in revenue, a 4% growth over last year, but below the RMB 246 billion consensus forecast. In US dollars, revenue came in at $33.94 billion, which is also up 4% YoY but falls short of the $34.6 billion expected. Non-GAAP earnings per share of RMB 2.05 was down 9% YoY, but normalized $2.29 per ADS beat the consensus of $2.09, according to Seeking Alpha Premium. So we could see that the economic problems of China - such as high unemployment and declining consumer demand - continued to hit Alibaba's main e-commerce business. On the other hand, Wall Street analysts started to revise their forecasts for Q2 FY2025 more actively, which is generally a good sign:

Breaking down the segments' performance, we see that the Alibaba Chinese retail business (including Taobao and Tmall Group) went down by ~1% to RMB 107.4 billion, now accounting for ~47% of total revenue. This was mainly due to a 9% decline in direct sales despite a 1% increase in customer management. Adjusted EBITDA for this business declined 1% to RMB 48.8 billion, and EBITDA margin slightly rose to 43.1%. The strategic shift from a user-centric perspective and pricing wars have put pressure on margins, which typically fell between 40-60%. However, Alibaba's management now expects that higher GMV will eventually increase revenue and margins. Prospective estimates are what we should care about the most, as the stock prices usually reflect the forwarding projections, not the past results.

The international commerce segment remained Alibaba's star, with revenues up 32% to RMB 29.3 billion year-on-year, fueled by a 38% increase in international retail sales. Yet, this area recorded a negative margin of -12.7% which is a drastic decline from -1.9% at the same time last year, but I believe as the GMV expands, the margins should follow eventually.

Alibaba Cloud, another major area of interest for us, experienced a 6% growth in revenue at RMB 26.6 billion. Alibaba Cloud is focusing on increasing revenue quality by eliminating lower-margin contracts and taking advantage of the strong demand for public cloud and AI solutions. We already see some initial successes: Cloud's EBITA jumped 155% YoY, and margins increased from 3.6% to 8.8%. I believe more to come, as the Western peers tend to earn times more in terms of margins (Amazon's (AMZN) AWS makes 38% in EBIT margin, for example).

In spite of these growth opportunities, in the last few years, Alibaba has struggled a lot, especially in the e-commerce sector. It's been falling short in market share to its peers such as PDD Holdings (PDD) and Douyin, who have penetrated into Alibaba's longtime areas of dominance: beauty and fashion. Between March 2022 and March 2023, Alibaba's market share in China's online retail sales of merchandise declined from 72% to 62%, according to Morningstar Premium data (proprietary source, August 2024). This is due to increased competition and the move to Taobao, which has a lower take rate than Tmall. In the long term, these competition pressures will likely drive down the company's monetization rates. But I think the GMV growth should smooth out the negative effect over time. In addition to that, the cloud opportunity, as well as the international commerce's marginality expansion should make BABA's financials stable enough for its management to keep rewarding the shareholders through buybacks and dividends.

Alibaba's balance sheet looks quite healthy, in my opinion, with high cash resources and a strong cash flow mix from its e-commerce stores. The company is announcing a large share buyback program that it expects to expand by $25 billion to $35.3 billion over the next 3 financial years. This move is in line with the wider strategy to drive ROIC and remit more capital to shareholders. So this way, based on my calculations, Alibaba may repurchase nearly 15% of its market capitalization from the market over the next 3 years. Coupled with a small yet stable dividend of over 1%, this strategy should provide a comfortably attractive total shareholder return.

I believe Alibaba's economic moat, the combination of a strong network effect and operational expertise, hasn't been demolished in this competitive environment. The company is still monetizing its platform well and still has higher adjusted EBIT margins than other players such as JD.com (JD) and Pinduoduo.

For fiscal Q2 FY2025, BABA forecasts a gradual alignment of its core China commerce revenue with GMV growth. Alibaba also predicts strong revenue growth in the cloud, which will be driven by AI-related and core public cloud demand. The company is also geared towards streamlining its services and improving cross-selling between public cloud and AI solutions. So despite the difficult macroeconomic conditions in China, which are now expected to improve over the next few years, Alibaba remains determined to improve and capitalize on growth in the cloud and outside China.

Despite its strong growth over the past several weeks, the BABA stock remains quite undervalued, in my view. Although its free cash flow to market cap ratio (i.e., FCF yield) has declined from ~16% at the beginning of the year to ~9.4% today, the total cash on the company's balance sheet still exceeds 35% of its market cap. This suggests that the market continues to underestimate the company and remains skeptical of its recovery potential.

Meanwhile, if you examine Seeking Alpha Quant rating concerning Alibaba's Valuation, you'll notice it has shifted over the past 3 months indeed (while it's not overly negative, BABA has become slightly more expensive). However, according to the key valuation metrics I focus on (I highlighted them below), it remains quite undervalued. For instance, the forward PEG ratio, which accounts for P/E weighted on future EPS growth rate, indicates an undervaluation of nearly 28%. Additionally, the company's forwarding EV/EBITDA is 34% below the sector median. So I believe Alibaba would need to grow by about this amount to eliminate its margin of safety.

I think we might see an additional catalyst in the form of the company's fiscal Q2 report (scheduled for release on November 15, 2024) to see the recovery's continuation. Analysts are expecting BABA's EPS to decline by almost 5% YoY, according to Seeking Alpha data. Furthermore, recent changes in EPS estimates suggest that Wall Street has become increasingly cautious about the company's EPS growth prospects through the end of fiscal 2025:

I believe that, given the positive internal factors I've outlined above, the current environment is favorable for Alibaba to exceed analysts' forecasts in Q2 at least. In my opinion, this could serve as a positive catalyst, potentially leading to a continued recovery in the medium term.

Risks To My Thesis

One risk lies at the macroeconomic level. The incentives recently announced by the Chinese Communist Party are a strong catalyst for market growth, as they're expected to boost retail investor activity in the mainland's stock market and stimulate the real estate sector. However, it's important to remember that the debt burden in the Chinese real estate sector remains a major problem. So those incentives may only address the symptoms and not the causes of the economic challenges. If this is the case, I don't believe that the negative effects plaguing the Chinese economy will disappear anytime soon.

The second risk relates to strong competition and Alibaba's role in its niche markets. While the company's broad diversification and growth in certain segments paint an overall positive picture, it can't be ignored that Alibaba continues to lose market share to faster-growing Chinese companies in its key areas. If this trend continues, its stock price could stagnate or even fall further.

I also come to the conclusion that the company is undervalued. In my opinion, however, undervaluation alone is not a sufficient reason to buy the stock. Alibaba has remained cheap over the last two years, but this has not prevented the stock price from falling further and becoming increasingly undervalued. So there is a risk that this trend may continue.

Your Takeaway

Despite the numerous risks mentioned above, I believe that investors should consider adding to their positions in Alibaba stock after it has fallen slightly since the beginning of October. Although the company is indeed facing difficult times, I believe it has a clear plan for developing its key segments, increasing GMV, and improving profitability in areas that are currently unprofitable or marginally profitable. Given the scale and moat of the company and the significant investment it is putting into its further growth, I believe it's inevitable that management will succeed in these efforts. This should result in the fundamentals eventually becoming a driving force for the stock's growth in the medium term. The undervaluation that I identified about 3 months ago has decreased slightly but is still significant enough to warrant investors' attention. Buying the stock today could still provide a margin of safety, in my view.

With all of this in mind, I believe the stock will continue to rally over the medium term, and the company's fiscal second-quarter report could serve as a catalyst for that rally, especially if it beats forecasts as I expect it.

Good luck with your investments!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
